heading: Step 3: Create the index.
body:
**Input:**

```shell
python create_index.py
```

**Output:**

```
New index name: default
```

The MongoDB Search `date` type takes the following parameter:

| Option | Type   | Necessity | Description                                                               |
| ------ | ------ | --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| type   | string | required  | Human-readable label that identifies this field type. Value must be date. |

The following index definition examples use collections in the
[sample data sets](available-sample-datasets). After you
[Load Sample Data Into Atlas](load-sample-data), you can use
these examples to index fields as the dateFacet type.

Basic Example

The following example index definition indexes the  `released`
field as the MongoDB Search `date` type to support queries against that
field using MongoDB Search operators such as [near](near-ref),
[range](range-ref), and [equals](equals-ref).

```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["sample_mflix"]
collection = database["movies"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": False,
            "fields": {
                "released":{
                   "type": "date"
                }
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")
```

Multiple Types Example

The following example index definition indexes the  `released`
field as the `date` and `dateFacet` types to return the
following types of results for your queries:

- Search results for queries using MongoDB Search operators like
  [near](near-ref), [equals](equals-ref), and [range](range-ref).

- Metadata results for queries using MongoDB Search [faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref).

```python
from pymongo import MongoClient
from pymongo.operations import SearchIndexModel

# connect to your Atlas deployment
uri = "<connection-string>"
client = MongoClient(uri)

# set namespace
database = client["sample_mflix"]
collection = database["movies"]

# define your MongoDB Search index
search_index_model = SearchIndexModel(
    definition={
        "mappings": {
            "dynamic": False,
            "fields": {
                "released": [
                    {
                        "type": "date"
                    },
                    {
                        "type": "dateFacet"
                    }
                ]
            }
        },
    },
    name="default",
)

# create the index
result = collection.create_search_index(model=search_index_model)
print(f"New index name: {result}")
```

To learn how to set up and run the following example code, see
Define the Index for the token Type.

You can use the MongoDB Search `date` type to index date values. You can query
fields of type `date` using the MongoDB Search [range](range-ref),
[near](near-ref), [in](in-ref), and [equals](equals-ref) operators. You can
also run a [faceted navigation](fts-facet-ref) query on `date` type fields.

You can also use the `date` type to index:

- Fields whose value is an array of dates. To learn more, see
  [How to Index the Elements of an Array](fts-array-ref).

- Date fields inside an array of documents indexed as the
  [embeddedDocuments](bson-data-types-embedded-documents) type.

- Date fields for sorting. MongoDB Search automatically indexes all date fields
  in indexes created after [July 2023](fts20230710) for
  [sorting](sort-ref) the MongoDB Search results. For preexisting indexes,
  you must rebuild the index to use date fields in the index for
  sorting. To learn more, see [index update](fts-index-rebuild) and
  [sorting](sort-ref).

> **Note:** To query indexed date values inside arrays, you must use the [range](range-ref)
> operator. You can't use the [near](near-ref) operator
> to query date values stored in an array, even if you have a MongoDB Search
> index on the date values inside the array.

If you enable [dynamic mappings](static-dynamic-mappings), MongoDB Search
automatically indexes fields of type dateFacet. You can
use the **Visual Editor** or the  **JSON Editor** in the Atlas UI to
index fields as the dateFacet type.
